doctrines.rule: An agreement to commit a crime may be inferred from coordinated conduct and surrounding circumstances even in the absence of an explicit verbal or written pact.
doctrines.split: Alvarez Panel (mere presence/assistance insufficient) vs. Alvarez En Banc (promise to assist + surrounding circumstances sufficient to infer agreement).
levers: Spikes in communication during key events
Financial transfers following criminal acts
Presence at key locations via cell data
Absence of a plausible innocent explanation for coordination
traps: Students assume a formal 'meeting of the minds' is required for agreement.
Students confuse 'mere presence' at a crime scene with 'coordinated action' (Alvarez).

doctrines.rule: A defendant must act with the specific purpose of promoting or facilitating the commission of the object crime, regardless of the mental state required for the object crime itself.
doctrines.split: Some jurisdictions allow knowledge for 'aggravated' crimes (Lauria), but purpose is the standard rule.
levers: Defendant's stake in the venture
Inflated pricing for services
Gravity of the target offense (misdemeanor vs. felony)
traps: Applying a 'reckless' or 'negligent' standard to the agreement.
Confusing knowledge of a crime with intent to join it.

doctrines.rule: A conspirator or coconspirator must perform an act in pursuance of the conspiracy, though the act itself may be entirely legal and trivial.
doctrines.split: MPC requires an overt act only for 3rd, 4th, or 5th degree felonies; 18 U.S.C. § 371 requires it for all.
levers: Legality of the act (e.g., buying a plane ticket)
Remoteness of the act from the crime
Whether the act was performed by the defendant or a coconspirator
traps: Requiring a 'substantial step' as in attempt law rather than a 'trivial' overt act.
Thinking the defendant themselves must perform the act (any member's act suffices).

doctrines.rule: Under the unilateral approach, a conspiracy exists if the defendant believes they are agreeing with another, even if the other party is a feigning government agent.
doctrines.split: Traditional/Bilateral (requires two genuine minds) vs. MPC/Unilateral (focuses on defendant's belief).
levers: Status of the coconspirator (undercover agent vs. genuine criminal)
Defendant's belief in the genuineness of the agreement
traps: Applying the bilateral rule in an MPC jurisdiction to dismiss a sting operation charge.
Failing to distinguish between the agent's intent and the defendant's intent.

doctrines.rule: A conspirator must specifically intend that every element of the target offense be accomplished, including aggravating factors like weapon use.
doctrines.split: Pond (requires intent for all elements) vs. Espinosa Dissent (only requires the mens rea of the target offense).
levers: Defendant's knowledge of a weapon
Agreement to 'scare' vs. agreement to 'rob'
Whether the element is an aggravating factor or a core element
traps: Assuming conspiracy to commit a strict liability crime doesn't require intent for the strict liability element.
Failing to apply the Pond rule to lookout defendants who didn't know about a weapon.

doctrines.rule: Conspiracy cannot be maintained for crimes defined by an unintended result, such as crimes of negligence.
levers: Nature of the target offense (negligent homicide vs. intentional murder)
Whether the result was intended or accidental
traps: Charging conspiracy to commit involuntary manslaughter.
Assuming one can 'agree' to be negligent.

doctrines.rule: A supplier of legal goods/services is not a conspirator based on mere knowledge of criminal use unless they have a 'stake in the venture' or the crime is 'aggravated.'
doctrines.split: Falcone (requires stake in venture) vs. Lauria (allows inference from gravity of crime).
levers: Inflated prices
Volume of business
Legitimacy of other customers
Gravity of crime (misdemeanor vs. felony)
traps: Assuming high-volume sales to criminals automatically equals conspiracy.
Failing to distinguish between a 'stake' (profit-sharing) and 'mere knowledge'.

doctrines.rule: A conspirator is liable for substantive crimes committed by coconspirators if the crimes were foreseeable and in furtherance of the conspiracy.
doctrines.split: Federal/Pinkerton (vicarious liability) vs. MPC (rejects Pinkerton; requires accomplice liability).
levers: Foreseeability of the side-crime
Whether the defendant was in custody/prison at the time
Whether the act furthered the specific goal
traps: Applying Pinkerton in an MPC jurisdiction.
Failing to check if the crime was a 'natural consequence' or a 'frolic' of the coconspirator.

doctrines.rule: A single conspiracy requires a 'rim' connecting the 'spokes' of a wheel—a common purpose or mutual stake—rather than just a common central figure.
levers: Knowledge of other members
Interdependence of transactions
Commonality of the goal
traps: Treating multiple independent frauds as one conspiracy because they use the same broker (rimless wheel).
Failing to identify the 'prejudice' of transference of guilt in a joint trial.

doctrines.rule: A simple agreement to buy or sell contraband for personal use does not constitute a conspiracy without evidence of a shared purpose to further distribution.
levers: Credit arrangements (fronting)
Prolonged course of dealing
Use of coded language
traps: Charging a one-time buyer of drugs with conspiracy to distribute.
Failing to see 'fronting' as evidence of mutual investment.

doctrines.rule: An agreement to commit a crime that by definition requires two participants (e.g., adultery, bribery) cannot be prosecuted as conspiracy unless additional parties are involved.
doctrines.split: Judicial presumption; can be overridden by clear legislative intent (Iannelli).
levers: Number of participants relative to the minimum required for the crime
Legislative intent to punish the agreement separately
traps: Applying the rule to crimes that *could* be committed by one person but happened to involve two.
Failing to count 'extra' parties that negate the rule.

doctrines.rule: A person belonging to a class protected or deliberately excluded by a statute cannot be prosecuted for conspiracy to violate that statute.
levers: Statutory purpose (e.g., protecting victims of trafficking)
Class of the defendant (e.g., the transported woman in Gebardi)
traps: Charging a minor with conspiracy to violate a law protecting minors.
Charging a victim with conspiracy to commit their own victimization.

doctrines.rule: Withdrawal requires an affirmative act to defeat the conspiracy and communication to all coconspirators; it terminates future liability but not the conspiracy charge itself.
doctrines.split: Common Law (Withdrawal only cuts off future crimes) vs. MPC (Renunciation is a complete defense if the conspirator thwarts the success).
levers: Communication to coconspirators
Efforts to thwart the crime
Timing of the withdrawal relative to the overt act
traps: Students assume withdrawal is a complete defense to the conspiracy charge itself in a non-MPC jurisdiction.
Failing to distinguish between 'withdrawal' and 'renunciation'.

doctrines.rule: Under the MPC, a defendant cannot be convicted of both conspiracy and the target offense; at common law, conspiracy does not merge.
levers: Whether the target crime was completed
Whether the conspiracy had multiple criminal objectives
traps: Applying merger to conspiracy in a common law jurisdiction.
Failing to realize conspiracy is a distinct offense from the target crime.

doctrines.rule: A single conspiracy exists where parties are links in a chain (e.g., manufacturer to retailer) and each knows the success of their part depends on the success of the whole.
levers: Mutual awareness of other links
Interdependence of the criminal enterprise
traps: Treating a chain as multiple conspiracies because the manufacturer never met the retailer.

cases.name: State v. Pond
cases.holding: Conspiracy requires specific intent for every element of the target offense, including aggravating factors like the display of a weapon.
cases.facts_summary: Defendant acted as a lookout for a robbery where a coconspirator gestured as if he had a gun; defendant claimed no knowledge or intent regarding the weapon.

cases.name: People v. Lauria
cases.holding: Knowledge of a customer's criminal activity is insufficient for conspiracy liability unless the supplier has a stake in the venture or the crime is aggravated.
cases.facts_summary: An answering service owner knew some of his clients were prostitutes but charged them standard rates and had no other involvement.

cases.name: Pinkerton v. United States
cases.holding: A conspirator is vicariously liable for substantive offenses committed by coconspirators in furtherance of the conspiracy and within its foreseeable scope.
cases.facts_summary: One brother committed tax evasion while the other was in prison; the imprisoned brother was held liable for the substantive crimes based on their ongoing conspiracy.

cases.name: Interstate Circuit v. United States
cases.holding: Agreement can be inferred where competitors adhere to a proposed plan knowing that concerted action is required for the plan's success.
cases.facts_summary: Movie distributors all received a letter naming each other as recipients and simultaneously changed their business practices to match the letter's demands.

cases.name: Kotteakos v. United States
cases.holding: A single conspiracy cannot be charged when multiple groups deal independently with a central figure without a common purpose or mutual connection (a 'rimless wheel').
cases.facts_summary: Thirty-two defendants were charged in one conspiracy for obtaining fraudulent loans through the same broker, despite not knowing each other.

cases.name: United States v. Alvarez
cases.holding: A promise to be at a drug unloading site, combined with surrounding circumstances, is sufficient for a jury to infer an agreement to participate in a conspiracy.
cases.facts_summary: Defendant nodded and smiled when asked if he would be at a marijuana unloading site and unloaded appliances from a truck at an airport.

cases.name: United States v. Falcone
cases.holding: A seller of goods who knows the buyer is using them for an illegal purpose is not guilty of conspiracy unless he has a 'stake in the venture.'
cases.facts_summary: Supplier sold sugar and yeast to bootleggers knowing they would make illegal alcohol.

cases.name: Gebardi v. United States
cases.holding: A woman who voluntarily consents to being transported across state lines for immoral purposes cannot be convicted of conspiracy to violate the Mann Act because the statute excludes her from liability.
cases.facts_summary: A man and woman were charged with conspiracy to violate the Mann Act after traveling together voluntarily.

cases.name: Iannelli v. United States
cases.holding: Wharton's Rule is a judicial presumption that can be overridden by clear legislative intent to punish conspiracy separately from the substantive offense.
cases.facts_summary: Defendants were charged with conspiracy to violate a federal gambling statute that required five or more persons.

cases.name: United States v. Pauling
cases.holding: A mere agreement to purchase drugs for personal use or resale does not establish a conspiracy without evidence of a shared purpose to further distribution.
cases.facts_summary: Analysis of the buyer-seller exception in drug distribution cases.

blocks.summary: The Dan Markel murder-for-hire case study illustrating inference of agreement, chain structure, and vicarious liability.
MPC 5.03 and 18 U.S.C. 371 defining conspiracy and the overt act requirement.
Breakdown of conspiracy elements: agreement, complex mental state, and minimal overt act.
Analysis of the split between bilateral and unilateral conspiracy theories in the context of undercover stings.
State v. Pond (majority and dissent) regarding the requirement of specific intent for all elements of the object crime.
People v. Koenig and the bar on conspiracy for crimes of negligence.
People v. Lauria and the distinction between knowledge and purpose for commercial suppliers.
Discussion of Purdue Pharma and social media platforms (Silk Road vs. Facebook) under the Lauria factors.
Pinkerton v. United States establishing vicarious liability for coconspirators and the withdrawal standard.
Bank robbery hypothetical and the MPC rejection of Pinkerton liability.
Interstate Circuit v. United States regarding conscious parallelism and inferred agreement among distributors.
Kotteakos v. United States and the 'rimless wheel' problem in large-scale prosecutions.
United States v. Alvarez and the outer limits of inferred agreement from a nod and a smile.
The buyer-seller exception in drug cases and the hallmarks of shared purpose (fronting).
Structural limits on conspiracy including Wharton's Rule and the Gebardi legislative exemption principle.
Discussion of corporate conspiracy and the Purdue Pharma case study.
Analysis of drug stings, manufactured conspiracy, and the 'girlfriend problem' in sentencing.
System architecture critique involving asset forfeiture, proactive policing, and MPC reforms.

hypo_seeds.description: A lookout in a robbery where a partner unexpectedly uses a toy gun that looks real.
hypo_seeds.note: Tests whether the student applies the Pond rule requiring specific intent for the 'display' element.

hypo_seeds.description: A chemical supplier sells large quantities of precursor chemicals to a known drug lab at 3x market price.
hypo_seeds.note: Tests the 'stake in the venture' (inflated prices) inference from Lauria.

hypo_seeds.description: A ringleader hires two separate hitmen who don't know each other to kill the same target.
hypo_seeds.note: Tests the 'rim' requirement—is this one conspiracy or two?

hypo_seeds.description: A defendant agrees with an undercover cop to buy stolen goods, but the goods aren't actually stolen.
hypo_seeds.note: Tests the bilateral vs. unilateral split regarding feigning coconspirators.

hypo_seeds.description: A social media platform algorithm amplifies drug trafficking content because it drives engagement and ad revenue.
hypo_seeds.note: Tests whether engagement-driven revenue constitutes a 'stake in the venture' under Lauria.

hypo_seeds.description: A defendant agrees to a bank robbery but is arrested for a separate crime and is in jail when the robbery occurs.
hypo_seeds.note: Tests whether incarceration constitutes withdrawal from a conspiracy (Pinkerton facts).
